teamObjective.explanation: As the opening leader with a weak, non-trump hand, I want to win the first trick with my strongest card to control the tempo and avoid handing the lead cheaply to the opponent who is down to 2 cards.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) holds 3 cards and has not acted, so their strength is unknown. They are likely to cover a low lead if they have a higher card or trump, but I cannot assume they have one.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponent player 1 has only 2 cards and is likely trying to finish quickly, so they may hold a trump or high card. Opponent player 3 has 3 cards and may help block or set up player 1. No cards have been played, so these are probabilistic.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 6 of clubs gives the best chance to win the trick among my legal singles, because it is the highest card I hold. If it wins, I keep the lead and can continue applying pressure; if an opponent beats it, they must use a higher non-trump or a valuable trump, which slows down the short-stacked opponent.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Leading the 3 is more likely to be beaten by a modest opponent card, which would transfer the lead directly to the opponent closest to finishing. That risk outweighs the signaling benefit at this early, information-scarce point.
The 4 is still low enough that many opponent holdings can beat it without using premium cards, so it does not sufficiently protect against the near-finish opponent taking control.
The 5 is better than 3 or 4, but the 6 still provides the highest probability of winning or forcing a stronger response, so it is the preferred opening lead.

risks: An opponent may have a trump or higher non-trump and win the trick, giving the short-stacked opponent the lead.
Partner may be unable to cover, leaving us with no immediate way to stop a fast finish.
Even if the 6 wins, my remaining cards are lower and may be beaten on subsequent tricks.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading my highest card first, I either win the trick or force opponents to spend meaningful strength; this is the least costly way to contest tempo given my weak hand and the opponent's low card count.

notes: Decision made under high uncertainty at game start with only public hand counts observed.
